content.text: TypedDict and Optional are two important type constructs in Python's type annotation system that handle two distinct but common patterns: typed dictionaries and values that may be absent.
TypedDict (introduced in PEP 589, Python 3.8) allows annotating Python dictionaries with known, typed keys. Unlike a plain

, a TypedDict declaration specifies exactly which keys exist, their types, and — optionally — which keys are required versus optional. This makes TypedDict the standard way to type structured data that arrives as JSON, configuration files, API responses, or any data that is naturally represented as key-value maps in Python.
Optional is a type alias from Python's
. It expresses that a value may be either of type X or absent (None). In Python 3.10+, the same concept is written as
using the pipe union syntax. Optional is used whenever a function might not return a value, a configuration field might not be set, or a database lookup might find nothing.

These two constructs are foundational to real-world Python typing: nearly every non-trivial codebase deals with optional fields in configuration and API payloads, and many systems receive structured data as JSON dictionaries that need type-safe access. TypedDict and Optional together let you model these patterns correctly in the type system.
Untyped dictionaries are one of the most common sources of runtime AttributeError and KeyError in Python. When a function receives a

, the type checker cannot verify that 'username' is a valid key. If the dictionary schema changes or a caller passes a different format, the access fails at runtime rather than at static analysis time.
TypedDict eliminates this class of error by giving the type checker enough information to verify key accesses, catch missing required fields, and prevent accessing undefined keys. Code that accesses
where user is typed as a UserProfile TypedDict is verified by mypy: if 'username' is not in UserProfile, mypy reports an error immediately.

Optional is equally important for correctness. Python functions that look up a database record, search a collection, or parse an optional configuration field commonly return None to signal 'not found'. Without Optional typing, callers may forget to check for None and call a method on it, causing the dreaded AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute '...'. Typing the return as
forces mypy to report an error on any code that uses the return value without first checking for None.

TypedDict verifies that accessed keys are declared, that values have the correct type, and that all required keys are present when creating the dict. No more guessing whether 'user_id' or 'userId' is the right key.
TypedDict is the natural type for JSON objects that arrive as Python dicts. Parsing an API response into a TypedDict-annotated variable gives every downstream access full type information.

Using Required and NotRequired (Python 3.11+) or total=False, you can precisely specify which dict keys must be present and which may be absent. This models JSON schemas and configuration formats exactly.
Optional forces mypy to flag any code that uses a possibly-None value without a None check. This catches entire classes of AttributeError at static analysis time.
guard, mypy narrows the type from
automatically, enabling safe use of value inside the guard block.
TypedDicts can inherit from other TypedDicts, allowing you to model base types and extensions just as you would with class inheritance.
Like all type hints, TypedDict annotations are not enforced by Python itself. A function annotated to accept a UserProfile TypedDict will happily accept any
at runtime; only mypy catches mismatches.

TypedDict values are plain Python dicts, not class instances. They have no methods, no properties, and no encapsulation. For anything beyond simple data transfer, a dataclass or Pydantic model is more appropriate.

Annotating too many values as Optional dilutes its signal value and forces callers to handle None everywhere. Consider whether a value is truly optional or whether it should always be present (and the caller should be responsible for ensuring it).
The interplay of total=True, total=False, Required, and NotRequired can become confusing in complex TypedDict hierarchies. Prefer explicit NotRequired over total=False for clarity.
TypedDict does not validate that a dictionary at runtime actually has the correct keys and types. For runtime enforcement, use Pydantic's BaseModel, which also supports dict-like access.
TypedDict can be defined using the class syntax (recommended) or the functional syntax. Both produce the same result but the class syntax is more readable and supports subclassing.
By default, all keys in a TypedDict are required (
type qualifiers (Python 3.11+; also in typing_extensions for older versions) allow fine-grained control of individual keys in a TypedDict with any
Mypy treats a TypedDict as a structural type: any dict literal that has all the required keys with the correct value types, and no extra keys, satisfies the TypedDict. Mypy checks key access (
) against the declared keys: accessing an undeclared key is a type error. Mypy also checks that all required keys are present when a TypedDict is constructed as a dict literal.
When a variable is typed as
), mypy knows it can be either X or None. Operations that require X (like attribute access or method calls) are errors until mypy can narrow the type to X. Narrowing occurs through:
narrows to X inside the block.
narrows to X (for types where None is falsy), though this is less precise.
narrows for the rest of the function.
if value is None: raise ValueError('...')
narrows for the rest of the function after the conditional.
The diagram below shows a TypedDict definition with required and optional keys, and how Optional drives type narrowing through a guard pattern.

attrs.alt: TypedDict structure and Optional type narrowing diagram

content.text: is declared with NotRequired and may be absent. In the Optional panel, the narrowing pattern transforms
inside an if-not-None guard, enabling safe string operations.
Use TypedDict for structured data that naturally arrives as JSON or as Python dict literals — API responses, configuration files, database row dictionaries, argparse Namespace-like structures.
Use TypedDict (rather than a class or dataclass) when the data must remain a plain dict for interoperability with libraries that accept dicts — JSON serialization, database drivers,
Use Optional[X] for any function parameter or return value that can legitimately be absent. The None case should carry semantic meaning ('not found', 'not set', 'default to something else').
(Python 3.10+ syntax) instead of Optional[X] for new code — it is clearer and requires one fewer import.

Avoid Optional when you can return an empty collection, a sentinel object, or raise an exception instead. Optional is correct when None is the natural representation of 'nothing', not when it is used as a substitute for a proper empty-state type.
Prefer Pydantic BaseModel over TypedDict when you need runtime validation, default values computed from other fields, validators, or methods on the data object.

from typing import TypedDict, Optional, Required, NotRequired
from typing import Union

# ─── 1. Basic TypedDict ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class UserProfile(TypedDict):
    """Represents a user profile as returned by the API."""
    id: int
    username: str
    email: str
    age: int

# Creating a UserProfile dict literal — mypy checks all required keys
user: UserProfile = {
    "id": 42,
    "username": "alice",
    "email": "alice@example.com",
    "age": 30,
}

# Type-safe access:
print(user["username"])   # str — mypy knows this
# print(user["phone"])    # mypy error: "phone" is not a key of UserProfile

# ─── 2. TypedDict with optional keys (total=False) ────────────────────────────
class UserAddress(TypedDict, total=False):
    """All fields are optional — use when building a dict incrementally."""
    street: str
    city: str
    state: str
    zip_code: str

address: UserAddress = {}  # valid — all keys are optional
address["city"] = "San Francisco"
address["state"] = "CA"

# ─── 3. Mixed required and optional keys ─────────────────────────────────────
class BlogPost(TypedDict):
    title: str                          # required
    content: str                        # required
    author: str                         # required
    tags: NotRequired[list[str]]        # optional — may be absent
    published_at: NotRequired[str]      # optional — may be absent
    view_count: NotRequired[int]        # optional — may be absent

# Valid: only required keys present
minimal_post: BlogPost = {
    "title": "Hello World",
    "content": "My first post.",
    "author": "Alice",
}

# Also valid: with optional keys
full_post: BlogPost = {
    "title": "Advanced Python",
    "content": "Generics and Protocols...",
    "author": "Bob",
    "tags": ["python", "typing"],
    "published_at": "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z",
    "view_count": 0,
}

# ─── 4. TypedDict inheritance ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
class BaseEntity(TypedDict):
    id: int
    created_at: str
    updated_at: str

class Product(BaseEntity):
    name: str
    price: float
    description: NotRequired[str]

product: Product = {
    "id": 1,
    "created_at": "2024-01-01T00:00:00Z",
    "updated_at": "2024-01-10T00:00:00Z",
    "name": "Widget Pro",
    "price": 29.99,
}

from typing import TypedDict, Optional
import json
import urllib.request

# Typing a JSON API response structure
class GithubUser(TypedDict):
    login: str
    id: int
    name: Optional[str]
    bio: Optional[str]
    public_repos: int
    followers: int
    following: int
    created_at: str

class GithubRepo(TypedDict):
    id: int
    name: str
    full_name: str
    description: Optional[str]
    stargazers_count: int
    forks_count: int
    language: Optional[str]
    html_url: str

def fetch_github_user(username: str) -> GithubUser:
    """Fetch a GitHub user and return as a TypedDict."""
    url = f"https://api.github.com/users/{username}"
    with urllib.request.urlopen(url) as response:
        data = json.loads(response.read())
    # Cast dict to TypedDict — mypy trusts this explicit cast
    return data  # type: ignore[return-value]  # real code would validate

def summarize_user(user: GithubUser) -> str:
    """Produce a summary string from a typed user dict."""
    name = user["name"] or user["login"]  # Optional[str] fallback
    bio_part = f" — {user['bio']}" if user["bio"] else ""
    return (
        f"{name}{bio_part} | "
        f"{user['public_repos']} repos | "
        f"{user['followers']} followers"
    )

def filter_repos_by_language(
    repos: list[GithubRepo],
    language: str,
) -> list[GithubRepo]:
    """Return repos filtered to a specific language."""
    # TypedDict access is type-checked: r["language"] is Optional[str]
    return [r for r in repos if r["language"] == language]

# Demo (would call the real API in practice)
mock_user: GithubUser = {
    "login": "python",
    "id": 1234,
    "name": "Python Software Foundation",
    "bio": "The Python programming language",
    "public_repos": 45,
    "followers": 100000,
    "following": 0,
    "created_at": "2011-01-17T18:17:03Z",
}
print(summarize_user(mock_user))

from typing import TypedDict

# Functional syntax — useful when key names conflict with Python keywords
Config = TypedDict("Config", {
    "host": str,
    "port": int,
    "class": str,        # 'class' is a Python keyword, impossible in class syntax
    "debug": bool,
    "timeout": float,
})

# total=False in functional syntax
OptionalConfig = TypedDict("OptionalConfig", {
    "log_level": str,
    "max_connections": int,
    "retry_delay": float,
}, total=False)

# Splitting mandatory and optional fields using inheritance
class RequiredDBConfig(TypedDict):
    host: str
    port: int
    database: str

class OptionalDBConfig(RequiredDBConfig, total=False):
    user: str
    password: str
    ssl: bool
    connection_timeout: float

# Only required fields needed:
minimal: OptionalDBConfig = {
    "host": "localhost",
    "port": 5432,
    "database": "myapp",
}

# All fields:
full: OptionalDBConfig = {
    "host": "db.example.com",
    "port": 5432,
    "database": "production",
    "user": "app_user",
    "password": "s3cr3t",
    "ssl": True,
    "connection_timeout": 30.0,
}

print("Minimal config host:", minimal["host"])
print("Full config ssl:", full.get("ssl", False))  # .get() for optional keys

In production agentic AI systems, LLM responses that include structured data or tool calls often arrive as Python dicts (deserialized JSON). TypedDict is the right tool to type these structures. The following example shows a typed tool-call response handler:

This pattern is used in production LLM agent frameworks. Every key access on the TypedDict-annotated response dict is verified by mypy at static-analysis time. If the OpenAI API schema changes (e.g., 'finish_reason' renamed to 'stop_reason'), mypy immediately flags all access sites rather than waiting for runtime failures.

from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired

class EventPayload(TypedDict):
    event_type: str
    user_id: int
    metadata: NotRequired[dict[str, str]]

payload: EventPayload = {"event_type": "click", "user_id": 42}

# WRONG: direct access fails if key absent
try:
    meta = payload["metadata"]  # KeyError at runtime!
except KeyError as e:
    print(f"KeyError: {e}")

# CORRECT: use .get() for NotRequired keys
meta = payload.get("metadata")     # Returns None if absent
meta_with_default = payload.get("metadata", {})  # Or provide default

# Even better: check explicitly
if "metadata" in payload:
    meta2 = payload["metadata"]   # TypedDict key presence check
    print("Has metadata:", meta2)

# ALSO CORRECT in Python 3.11+: use TypedDict key access with walrus
if (m := payload.get("metadata")) is not None:
    print("Metadata:", m)

from typing import Optional

def get_config(key: str) -> Optional[str]:
    config = {"host": "localhost", "port": "5432"}
    return config.get(key)

# WRONG: calling a method on a potentially-None return
value = get_config("host")
# print(value.upper())  # mypy error: Item "None" of "str | None" has no attribute "upper"
# AttributeError at runtime if key not found

# CORRECT: check for None first
value = get_config("host")
if value is not None:
    print(value.upper())   # safe: value is str here

# OR: use a default
value2 = get_config("missing_key") or "default"
print(value2.upper())  # safe: value2 is str (empty string is also falsy — be careful)

# OR: use walrus
if (v := get_config("host")) is not None:
    print(v.upper())

# OR: use or with explicit default that avoids empty string issue
result = get_config("missing")
clean_result: str = result if result is not None else "fallback"
Mistake 3: Using total=False when only a few keys are optional

from typing import TypedDict

# WRONG: making ALL keys optional to accommodate a few optional ones
class BadConfig(TypedDict, total=False):
    host: str     # required but looks optional!
    port: int     # required but looks optional!
    debug: bool   # actually optional
    timeout: float  # actually optional

# Callers might create an empty dict and never set host/port:
empty: BadConfig = {}  # mypy accepts it even though host/port are needed
# This is semantically wrong — host and port are required

# CORRECT: use NotRequired for only the actually-optional keys
from typing import NotRequired

class GoodConfig(TypedDict):
    host: str               # required
    port: int               # required
    debug: NotRequired[bool]   # optional
    timeout: NotRequired[float]  # optional

minimal: GoodConfig = {"host": "localhost", "port": 8080}  # valid
# empty_bad: GoodConfig = {}  # mypy error: missing required keys host, port
Mistake 4: Using Optional for values that should always be present

from typing import Optional

# WRONG: marking critical fields Optional 'just in case'
class OrderItem(TypedDict):
    product_id: Optional[int]   # Should always be present!
    quantity: Optional[int]     # Should always be present!
    unit_price: Optional[float] # Should always be present!

# This forces every caller to handle None for values that are always provided,
# adding noise and masking real Optional fields.

# CORRECT: only use Optional where None genuinely represents "absent"
class CorrectOrderItem(TypedDict):
    product_id: int    # always present
    quantity: int      # always present
    unit_price: float  # always present
    discount: NotRequired[float]  # may be absent (no discount applied)
    notes: NotRequired[str]       # customer notes, may be absent

Use TypedDict for data transfer objects (DTOs) — structures that move data between components with a fixed, known schema. For objects with behavior (methods, computed properties), use dataclasses or Pydantic models.
makes ALL keys optional, which is rarely the right semantics;
check. Direct bracket access on a NotRequired key will raise KeyError at runtime if absent, even though mypy might not catch it.
Use type narrowing consistently: immediately after a function that returns
, write the None check and narrow the type before any use of the value. This documents the intent and prevents accidental None propagation.
in Python 3.10+ code — it is more readable and requires no import from typing.
For function parameters that can be absent, use
without a default. The former makes the parameter optional to pass; the latter still requires an argument.
Use Pydantic's BaseModel instead of TypedDict when you need runtime validation, computed defaults, or validator methods. TypedDict is only a typing construct; Pydantic validates at runtime.
TypedDicts are plain Python dicts at runtime. There is zero overhead from the TypedDict annotation itself — accessing
on a TypedDict is identical to accessing any other dict. The type annotations exist only for mypy and other static tools; at runtime, the
definition creates a TypedDict type object but all values are stored in regular dicts.
Optional values that may be None require an explicit check before use. When performance is critical and Optional checks are in a hot path, consider using a sentinel pattern instead:
For TypedDict-heavy code in hot loops, the access pattern matters more than the TypedDict itself. Repeated
lookups in a tight loop can be optimized by assigning the value to a local variable once at the top of the loop body, which is the same optimization you would apply to any dictionary.
Fully understands TypedDict, Required, NotRequired, and Optional narrowing. Run with --strict to catch Optional usage without None guards.
Excellent TypedDict and Optional support with fast incremental checking. The VS Code Pylance extension shows TypedDict key types on hover.
Backports Required, NotRequired, and other newer TypedDict features to Python 3.9 and earlier. pip install typing_extensions.
Pydantic's BaseModel provides runtime validation with a similar annotation style. Pydantic also supports converting TypedDicts to and from BaseModel via model_validate.
Extends Python dataclasses with JSON serialization, providing an alternative to TypedDict for structured JSON handling with class-based syntax.
dict with forward references resolved. Useful for runtime introspection of TypedDict fields.

import typing
from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired, get_type_hints

class Article(TypedDict):
    id: int
    title: str
    body: str
    author: NotRequired[str]
    tags: NotRequired[list[str]]

# Inspect TypedDict structure at runtime
hints = get_type_hints(Article, include_extras=True)
print("Type hints:", hints)

# Check which fields are required
# (TypedDict stores required keys in __required_keys__ and optional in __optional_keys__)
print("Required keys:", Article.__required_keys__)    # frozenset({'id', 'title', 'body'})
print("Optional keys:", Article.__optional_keys__)    # frozenset({'author', 'tags'})
print("Total (all required):", Article.__total__)     # True (because we used NotRequired selectively)

# Convert TypedDict to Pydantic model for runtime validation
from pydantic import BaseModel

class ArticleModel(BaseModel):
    id: int
    title: str
    body: str
    author: str = "Anonymous"
    tags: list[str] = []

# Pydantic validates at runtime; TypedDict only at static-check time
raw_data = {"id": 1, "title": "Hello", "body": "World", "author": "Alice"}
article = ArticleModel(**raw_data)
print(article)
TypedDict and Optional improve code reliability, but there are specific security and reliability pitfalls to watch for when using them in production systems.

TypedDict does not validate data at runtime. If an API response is annotated as a specific TypedDict type (via a cast or type: ignore), the code assumes the dict has the declared keys. Malformed responses can cause KeyError. Always validate external data with Pydantic or json-schema before casting to TypedDict.

If a function returns Optional to indicate 'not found', callers that ignore the None case will crash when the resource is actually not found. Prefer raising a specific exception (UserNotFoundError) over returning None when 'not found' is an error condition, not a normal outcome.
TypedDict fields that are lists or dicts (e.g.,

) do not have default values — the caller must supply them. Using mutable defaults in TypedDict construction is not possible via the normal class syntax, but careless dict literal construction can reuse the same list object across instances.
In a multi-layer architecture, a function that receives an Optional from one layer and passes it to another without checking can propagate None silently. Use mypy with strict Optional checking (
, enabled by default in strict mode) to catch this.

When constructing a TypedDict from user-supplied data, validate all values. A TypedDict annotation on a function parameter does not prevent a caller from passing a dict with unexpected or malicious values — only Pydantic or explicit validation code does.

Think of TypedDict as a named shape for a dictionary. Just as a blueprint describes the rooms of a house (name, type, required?), a TypedDict describes the keys of a dict (name, value type, required?). Any dict that matches the blueprint — has all the required rooms, each the right size — satisfies the TypedDict. The 'blueprint' is checked at design time (mypy) but not at build time (runtime).
Think of Optional as a 'maybe box'. A value of type
is a box that either contains a User or is empty (None). Before you can use the User inside, you must open the box and check that it is not empty. The guard pattern (
) is the act of opening the box. Once open and confirmed non-empty, you have a real User and can use it freely.
An important distinction: Optional is about the
being optional to pass. A function parameter declared as
still requires the caller to explicitly pass a value (even if that value is None). To make the parameter truly optional to pass (with a default), you must also provide a default value:
TypedDict annotates dict literals with specific key names and value types, enabling mypy to verify key access and catch missing required keys at static-check time, not at runtime.
All TypedDict keys are required by default (
on individual keys to mark them as optional, which is clearer than
TypedDicts are plain Python dicts at runtime — they carry zero overhead and are fully compatible with any library or API that accepts dicts.
in Python 3.10+) marks a value that may be None; mypy requires a None-check guard before any operation that assumes the value is non-None.
Optional on a parameter type does not give it a default value; use
to make a parameter both optional to pass and allowed to be None.
checks for NotRequired TypedDict keys at runtime; direct bracket access will raise KeyError if the key is absent.

For runtime validation of TypedDict-shaped data (e.g., from external APIs), use Pydantic's BaseModel, which validates types and required fields at construction time rather than relying purely on static checks.
